techniques.evidence_quote: a former contractor accessed one of Chegg's S3 databases using an AWS Root Credential
AWS access key that provided full administrative privileges over all data in the S3 databases ('AWS Root Credentials')
Chegg shared the AWS Root Credentials among its employees and even outside contractors

techniques.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
techniques.evidence_quote: failed to require employees and third-party contractors that access the S3 databases to use distinct access keys, instead permitting employees and contractors to use a single AWS access key
a former contractor accessed one of Chegg's S3 databases using an AWS Root Credential
the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform

attack_chain.description: Former contractor leveraged a widely shared AWS Root Credential — the same single key distributed to all employees and contractors — to authenticate to Chegg's AWS environment. No MFA was required and the key had never been rotated.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Chegg shared the AWS Root Credentials among its employees and even outside contractors

attack_chain.description: Using the root credential with full administrative privileges, the former contractor identified and located the S3 bucket(s) containing the sensitive user database of approximately 40 million records.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a former contractor accessed one of Chegg's S3 databases using an AWS Root Credential

attack_chain.description: The former contractor exfiltrated the entire user database from the S3 bucket, including ~40 million users' email addresses, names, passwords (MD5-hashed), and sensitive scholarship search data (religion, sexual orientation, disability status, etc.) stored in plain text.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform

initial_access_vector: Former contractor used a broadly shared, single AWS Root Credential (access key) that Chegg distributed to all employees and contractors, with no MFA, no key rotation, and no per-user access controls.
persistence_mechanism: Not applicable — the shared root credential itself served as permanent standing access; no additional persistence mechanism was needed or described.
privilege_escalation: No escalation required — the shared credential already carried full administrative (root) privileges over all S3 data: "a single AWS access key that provided full administrative privileges over all data in the S3 databases."
